Scenario.overview: Energy Sector Simple [The attackers employ basic techniques to gain initial access through phishing, execute malware via malicious links and files, establish persistence using a web shell, and finally impact the system by encrypting data with ransomware.]
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Email Systems, Command & Control Server, File System
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, File Creation (Mechanism), Reg.exe Utility, Web Shell, Data Encrypted

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attackers send phishing emails to trick employees into clicking on malicious links or opening attachments containing malware.

stage.name: Execution
stage.description: Victims directed to a website hosting malware like BazarLoader upon clicking the malicious link in the phishing email.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-635: Alternative Execution Due to Deceptive Filenames
stage.description: Email attachments execute malware such as Cobalt Strike beacons when opened.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC-650: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: If initial execution leads to system compromise, a web shell is deployed on a server to maintain persistent access.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Conti ransomware is deployed to encrypt critical data, demanding a ransom for decryption keys.

Scenario.summary: The energy sector faces a targeted phishing campaign by the C0015 group, employing basic but effective tactics to achieve initial access, execute malware, establish persistence, and ultimately impact operations by encrypting data with ransomware.
